content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 4, 1991


Charles Knutsen, Chair
Democratic Party of the
San Fernando Valley
9140 Van Nuys Blvd., Suite 205
Panorama City, CA  91402

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-90-698

Dear Mr. Knutsen:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on
behalf of the Democratic Party of the San Fernando Valley regard-
ing the restrictions on contributions imposed by the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹  You have requested information which is
of a general nature.  Accordingly, we are providing you with
informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329
(copy enclosed).²

QUESTION

     In view of the recent court decisions regarding Propositions
73 and 68, are recipient committees such as the Democratic Party
of the San Fernando Valley subject to contribution limitations?

CONCLUSION

     Generally, recipient committees such as the Democratic Party
of the San Fernando Valley are not subject to the contribution
limitations imposed by Propositions 73 and 68.  However, contribu-
tions to the committee received for purposes of making contribu-
tions to legislative candidates continue to be subject to the
contribution limits of Proposition 73.  Furthermore, restrictions


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.  (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-698
Page 2

may apply to contributions received from candidates based upon
application of the transfer prohibition of the Act.

FACTS

The Democratic Party of the San Fernando Valley is a quasi-
legal extension of the California Democratic Party. Although
chartered by the Los Angeles County Democratic Central Committee,
this general purpose committee operates as an umbrella
organization for all the democratic clubs and individual democrats
in the San Fernando Valley.

ANALYSIS

On June 7, 1988, California voters approved Proposition 73
which established limits on contributions to candidates, commit-
tees, and political parties.³ (Sections 85301, 85302, 85303 and
85305.) However, the United States District Court, Eastern
District of California, has ruled that the fiscal year limitations
on campaign contributions imposed by Proposition 73 are
unconstitutional.⁴ Service Employees International Union, AFL-
CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No. CIVS-
89-0433 LKK-JFM) ("SEIU").) Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303(a)
and (b) placed limitations on campaign contributions based on a
fiscal year regulatory scheme.⁵ Section 85304 prohibited

_________________________

³ On November 1, 1990, the Supreme Court of the State of
California issued its opinion in Taxpayers To Limit Campaign
Spending v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No.
S012016.) The court held that when two or more measures are
competing initiatives, such is the case with Proposition 73 and
Proposition 68, only the provisions of the measure receiving the
highest number of affirmative votes are enforceable. Thus,
because Proposition 73 received a higher number of votes than
Proposition 68, the contribution limitations of Proposition 68 are
not in effect at this time.

⁴ Section 85305 of Proposition 73 was not affected by the court
order. Section 85305 sets contribution limitations on special
elections and is based on a per election scheme, as opposed to
other Proposition 73 contribution limitations which were based on
the fiscal year scheme. Thus, the contribution limitations of
Section 85305 control with respect to special elections, whether
they are statewide special elections or special elections in local
jurisdictions.

⁵ Section 85302 limited campaign contributions to political com-
mittees to $2,500 in a fiscal year. Sections 85301 and 85303
provided that a "person" could contribute up to $1,000 in a fiscal
year to a candidate; a "political committee" could contribute
up to $2,500 in a fiscal year to a candidate; and a "broad based
political committee or political party" could contribute up to
$5,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate.

File No. I-90-698
Page 3

candidates and their controlled committees from transferring
campaign funds to other candidates. The court also invalidated
the transfer prohibitions of Section 85304 to the extent the
prohibitions served to further the contribution limitations
otherwise found invalid.6

On September 28, 1990, the court issued a partial stay of the
judgment in SEIU and reinstated the contribution limitations and
transfer prohibition with respect to legislative candidates.
Thus, as long as the SEIU stay remains in place, the contribution
limitations and ban on transfers remain applicable to legislative
candidates.7 This means that Section 85302, which limits
contributions to committees, still limits contributions to committees which are to be used to make contributions to legislative
candidates. This also means that if your organization receives a
contribution from a candidate or a committee controlled by a
candidate, your organization cannot use these funds to make
contributions to legislative candidates.8 Such a transaction
would be a violation of the transfer ban.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:plh

___________________________

6 The decision in SEIU has been appealed. Our advice is subject
to change pending the outcome of this appeal.

7 Regardless of the stay, the transfer ban also applies to local
candidates subject to valid local contribution limits.

8 These funds may be used for non-candidate related activities or
to make contributions to candidates not subject to the limits.
(Section 85303(c); Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-89-327; Martin
Advice Letter, No. A-89-301; copies enclosed.)

However, contributions to the committee received for purposes of making contributions to legislative candidates continue to be subject to the contribution limits of Proposition 73. sections.analysis:
On June 7, 1988, California voters approved Proposition 73 which established limits on contributions to candidates, committees, and political parties. (Sections 85301, 85302, 85303 and 85305.) However, the United States District Court, Eastern District of California, has ruled that the fiscal year limitations on campaign contributions imposed by Proposition 73 are unconstitutional. (Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No. CIVS-89-0433 LKK-JFM) ("SEIU").) Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303(a) and (b) placed limitations on campaign contributions based on a fiscal year regulatory scheme. Section 85304 prohibited

3 On November 1, 1990, the Supreme Court of the State of California issued its opinion in Taxpayers To Limit Campaign Spending v. Fair Political Practices Commission (Case No. S012016.) The court held that when two or more measures are competing initiatives, such as is the case with Proposition 73 and Proposition 68, only the provisions of the measure receiving the highest number of affirmative votes are enforceable. Thus, because Proposition 73 received a higher number of votes than Proposition 68, the contribution limitations of Proposition 68 are not in effect at this time.

4 Section 85305 of Proposition 73 was not affected by the court order. Section 85305 sets contribution limitations on special elections and is based on a per election scheme, as opposed to other Proposition 73 contribution limitations which were based on the fiscal year scheme. Thus, the contribution limitations of Section 85305 control with respect to special elections, whether they are statewide special elections or special elections in local jurisdictions.

5 Section 85302 limited campaign contributions to political committees to $2,500 in a fiscal year. Sections 85301 and 85303 provided that a "person" could contribute up to $1,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate; a "political committee" could contribute up to $2,500 in a fiscal year to a candidate; and a "broad based political committee or political party" could contribute up to $5,000 in a fiscal year to a candidate.

candidates and their controlled committees from transferring campaign funds to other candidates. The court also invalidated the transfer prohibitions of Section 85304 to the extent the prohibitions served to further the contribution limitations otherwise found invalid.6

On September 28, 1990, the court issued a partial stay of the judgment in SEIU and reinstated the contribution limitations and transfer prohibition with respect to legislative candidates. Thus, as long as the SEIU stay remains in place, the contribution limitations and ban on transfers remain applicable to legislative candidates.7 This means that Section 85302, which limits contributions to committees, still limits contributions to committees which are to be used to make contributions to legislative candidates. This also means that if your organization receives a contribution from a candidate or a committee controlled by a candidate, your organization cannot use these funds to make contributions to legislative candidates.8 Such a transaction would be a violation of the transfer ban.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.
